nodes.name: CI
nodes.summary: GitHub Actions workflow running on push and pull_request to main. Three independent jobs: a matrix test job (ubuntu-latest + windows-latest x Python 3.10/3.12/3.13 with pytest + coverage gate of >=60% and ruff lint/format checks), a mypy static type-checking job (ubuntu-latest, Python 3.13), and a dedicated frontend build job (ubuntu-latest, runs npm ci + npm run build in web-ui to enforce the Vue/TypeScript type-check + vite build as a regression guard added after the v1.17.9 numerical bug). The frontend is built before `pip install -e .[dev,web]` in every job because pyproject.toml's hatchling force-include requires web-ui/dist to exist.
nodes.languageNotes: YAML GitHub Actions workflow. Uses `matrix` strategy (os x python-version) with `fail-fast: false` so a Windows runner failure cannot mask a Linux runner failure (or vice versa). Triggers on push/PR to `main`. Pins action versions (`actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-python@v5`, `actions/setup-node@v4`) and uses `actions/setup-node` cache keyed on `web-ui/package-lock.json` for fast npm install. Each job sets `working-directory: web-ui` for npm steps so the cache-dependency-path and the working directory align.
nodes.languageLesson: Putting the frontend build inside every Python job (instead of once in a shared job) is the cheapest way to honor the hatchling force-include contract: each `pip install -e .` independently verifies web-ui/dist exists. The dedicated `frontend` job runs *only* the npm steps on ubuntu-latest so the matrix isn't repeated 6x for an OS- and Python-independent check.

nodes.name: Publish to PyPI
nodes.summary: GitHub Actions workflow that builds and publishes the package to PyPI when a version tag (v*) is pushed. Single job on ubuntu-latest in the `release` GitHub environment with OIDC trusted publishing (`id-token: write` + `attestations: true` for sigstore provenance). Steps: checkout -> setup-python 3.13 -> setup-node 20 -> npm ci + npm run build (web-ui) -> pip install build -> python -m build -> pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1. Building the frontend before `python -m build` is required because hatchling's force-include brings web-ui/dist into the wheel so `pip install easy-tdx[web]` ships a working UI out of the box.
nodes.languageNotes: YAML GitHub Actions workflow. Triggers exclusively on `tags: ['v*']` (no manual `workflow_dispatch`). Uses OIDC `id-token: write` permission so `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish@release/v1` can publish via PyPI trusted publishing without long-lived API tokens; `attestations: true` enables PEP 740 sigstore provenance so end users can cryptographically verify artifact origin.
nodes.languageLesson: OIDC trusted publishing + PEP 740 sigstore attestations removes both the need for a PyPI API token (no long-lived secret in repo secrets) and the trust gap on provenance (each install can verify which workflow + commit produced the wheel). This is the modern, recommended PyPI release flow.

nodes.name: Release EXE
nodes.summary: GitHub Actions workflow that builds a Windows single-file EXE via PyInstaller and publishes it as a GitHub Release asset when a `v*` tag is pushed. Two jobs: `build-windows` (windows-latest, Python 3.12, Node 20, builds web-ui, `pip install -e .[web,packaging]` + `pip install pyinstaller`, runs `pyinstaller easy_tdx.spec --noconfirm`, renames the output to `easy-tdx-${VERSION}-windows.exe` via shell, uploads as artifact) and `release` (ubuntu-latest, downloads the artifact, creates a GitHub Release via softprops/action-gh-release@v2 with auto-generated notes and a Chinese-language download guide that explains the SmartScreen bypass to elderly end users). Phase 1 produces unsigned binaries; Phase 2 will add code signing.
nodes.languageNotes: YAML GitHub Actions workflow with two jobs and an explicit `needs: build-windows` dependency. Sets `permissions: contents: write` so softprops/action-gh-release can create the release. The release body (Chinese) explains the unsigned-binary SmartScreen flow to elderly end users (`更多信息` -> `仍要运行`). Job order is load-bearing: frontend build -> `pip install -e .[web,packaging]` (force-include requires dist) -> pyinstaller -> rename via shell.
nodes.languageLesson: Splitting a release into two jobs that share work via `actions/upload-artifact` + `actions/download-artifact` lets the heavy Windows-only PyInstaller build happen once on `windows-latest` while the lighter GitHub Release publish runs on cheap `ubuntu-latest`. The same artifact flows across the runner boundary without checking it back into git.

edges.reason: release.yml's header comment explicitly notes that the EXE release runs in parallel with PyPI publishing to maximize release resilience: both share the `v*` tag trigger and a single failing workflow does not block the other.

summary.notes: All three files are GitHub Actions workflows (yaml, fileCategory=infra, node type `pipeline`). No source imports are resolved for these files (YAML infra does not import Python/TS modules) and the cross-batch NEIGHBOR_MAP was empty, so the only within-batch edge models the parallel `v*` tag-trigger relationship between release.yml and publish.yml explicitly documented in release.yml's header comment. All three workflows share an implicit upstream contract: web-ui/dist must be built before any `pip install -e .[web]` or `python -m build` step because pyproject.toml's hatchling force-include pulls web-ui/dist into the wheel; ci.yml even includes that npm build inside every Python job (test, mypy) and adds a dedicated frontend job to enforce the Vue/TypeScript type-check + vite build as a regression guard added after the v1.17.9 numerical bug. The release workflow is intentionally independent of the publish workflow (separate jobs, different runners) so that a failure in one (e.g. PyPI rejection, SmartScreen note mishandling) does not block the other.
